scenario: A friend forwards you an article headlined "Study proves vaccine causes long-term harm." The article cites "research from a leading institute" but does not name the institute or link to the study.

evidenceCards.title: Article: Study proves vaccine causes long-term harm
evidenceCards.snippet: Shares a headline and one paragraph; no author byline or date.

evidenceCards.title: WHO vaccine safety overview
evidenceCards.snippet: Summarises monitored adverse events and ongoing studies; links to published data.

options.text: Trust the article because it sounds scientific
options.explanation: The headline uses scientific language but provides no verifiable source.

options.text: Withhold judgment until you can find the original study and who funded it
options.explanation: Strong critical thinking: check primary sources and incentives before concluding.

options.text: Assume it is false because WHO exists
options.explanation: Dismissing a claim only because another body exists is not evidence-based evaluation.

options.text: Share it so others can decide for themselves
options.explanation: Sharing unverified claims can spread misinformation; verification should come first.

takeaway: Always identify the primary source and its incentives before accepting a claim.

scenario: You read two pieces about the same policy: one from a government press release and one from an opposition party's website. Both describe the same new law but draw opposite conclusions.

evidenceCards.title: Gov press release: New law strengthens accountability
evidenceCards.snippet: Emphasises transparency and public consultation in the new framework.

evidenceCards.title: Opposition: New law concentrates power
evidenceCards.snippet: Argues the law grants excessive discretion and weakens checks.

options.text: The government is always more reliable
options.explanation: Assuming one side is always right is bias, not reasoning.

options.text: Look for neutral analysis (e.g. academics, fact-checkers) and primary legal text
options.explanation: Seeking independent and primary sources helps balance partisan framing.

options.text: The opposition is always more critical and therefore more honest
options.explanation: Critical does not automatically mean accurate; evidence still matters.

options.text: Pick the side that matches your existing views
options.explanation: Confirmation bias undermines critical thinking.

takeaway: When stakeholders disagree, seek neutral analysis and primary sources.

scenario: Someone claims "Experts say X is dangerous." You find one op-ed by a retired professor who says X is dangerous, and three peer-reviewed studies that find no significant risk.

evidenceCards.title: Op-ed: Why X is dangerous
evidenceCards.snippet: Single author, retired academic; no new data, personal view.

evidenceCards.title: Meta-analysis in Journal of Y
evidenceCards.snippet: Systematic review of 24 studies; concludes no significant risk in normal use.

options.text: One expert said it so it must be true
options.explanation: One opinion does not outweigh systematic evidence.

options.text: Weight the peer-reviewed meta-analysis more than a single op-ed
options.explanation: Aggregated, peer-reviewed evidence is typically stronger than one opinion.

options.text: Ignore both and decide based on gut feeling
options.explanation: Evidence should inform conclusions rather than gut feeling.

options.text: Assume the op-ed is right because it is more readable
options.explanation: Clarity of writing does not indicate accuracy.

takeaway: Weigh evidence by type and volume: peer-reviewed synthesis usually outweighs single opinions.

scenario: A rumour says "Event A was cancelled because of secret pressure." You know Event A was cancelled; the organiser cited "logistical reasons."

evidenceCards.title: Organiser statement
evidenceCards.snippet: Event A is cancelled due to logistical constraints; we apologise for any inconvenience.

evidenceCards.title: Anonymous post
evidenceCards.snippet: Heard from a friend that A was cancelled after pressure from [entity]. No proof.

options.text: The secret pressure explanation is true because it is more interesting
options.explanation: Interest or drama does not make a claim true.

options.text: Treat the rumour as unconfirmed; look for corroborating evidence before believing
options.explanation: Extraordinary claims need corroboration; one anonymous post is weak.

options.text: The organiser would never tell the truth so the rumour is true
options.explanation: Assuming the opposite of an official statement is not sound reasoning.

options.text: Believe whichever you heard first
options.explanation: Order of exposure is not a valid way to weigh evidence.

takeaway: Unverified rumours need corroboration; don't assume the most dramatic explanation is correct.

scenario: A video clip shows a public figure saying something controversial. The clip is 15 seconds long and has been shared millions of times. The caption says they "admitted" something damaging.

evidenceCards.title: 15-second clip
evidenceCards.snippet: No context: you cannot see what question was asked or what was said before/after.

evidenceCards.title: Full press conference transcript
evidenceCards.snippet: Shows full Q&A; in context the remark was hypothetical and later clarified.

options.text: Trust the clip because it went viral
options.explanation: Virality does not indicate accuracy; clips can be misleading.

options.text: Seek the full context (full video or transcript) before judging
options.explanation: Short clips can distort meaning; full context is essential.

options.text: Assume the caption is correct
options.explanation: Captions are often interpretive or biased; verify against primary source.

options.text: Ignore the transcript if it contradicts the clip
options.explanation: Primary sources should override edited excerpts.

takeaway: Short clips can misrepresent; always seek full context before concluding.

scenario: You are reading an article that strongly supports a policy you already favour. The article quotes several experts and cites studies.

evidenceCards.title: Article in favour of policy
evidenceCards.snippet: Quotes 4 experts who support the policy; cites 2 studies with positive findings.

evidenceCards.title: Academic overview
evidenceCards.snippet: Summarises mixed evidence: benefits in X, limitations in Y, need for more data.

options.text: Accept the article because it matches your view
options.explanation: Confirmation bias: agreeing with a view is not evidence it is correct.

options.text: Seek out summaries that present mixed or opposing views to test your view
options.explanation: Seeking disconfirming evidence strengthens reasoning.

options.text: Dismiss the academic overview as too cautious
options.explanation: Nuance and uncertainty are part of honest evidence; don't dismiss them.

options.text: Share the article without checking other sources
options.explanation: Sharing one-sided content can reinforce bias in others.

takeaway: Actively seek out opposing or mixed evidence to avoid confirmation bias.

scenario: Someone says "A celebrity endorsed this product so it must work." You find the celebrity was paid to promote it and there are no independent studies on the product.

evidenceCards.title: Celebrity ad
evidenceCards.snippet: Celebrity says they use the product every day; small print: paid partnership.

evidenceCards.title: Independent testing
evidenceCards.snippet: No published tests found for this product; company did not provide samples.

options.text: Celebrity endorsement is strong evidence
options.explanation: Paid endorsements are not independent evidence of efficacy.

options.text: Treat the claim as unproven until there is independent evidence
options.explanation: Without independent testing, efficacy claims are not supported.

options.text: Assume it works because the celebrity is famous
options.explanation: Fame does not equal expertise or honesty in ads.

options.text: Assume it does not work because it is advertised
options.explanation: Skepticism is good but concluding the opposite without evidence is also weak.

takeaway: Paid endorsements are not evidence of effectiveness; look for independent evidence.

scenario: A message claims "They are hiding the real numbers." You find official statistics published on a government website, with methodology explained.

evidenceCards.title: Message
evidenceCards.snippet: They are hiding the real numbers. Everyone knows it.

evidenceCards.title: Official statistics
evidenceCards.snippet: Data tables, methodology note, and release calendar.

options.text: Believe the message because it is widely shared
options.explanation: Sharing does not substitute for evidence.

options.text: Treat the official data as the best available evidence unless you find specific flaws
options.explanation: Documented, methodologically clear data is stronger than unsupported claims.

options.text: Assume officials always lie
options.explanation: Assuming the opposite of official data without evidence is not reasoning.

options.text: Ignore both and form your own number
options.explanation: Inventing data does not improve accuracy.

takeaway: Unsupported claims of "hidden" data should be weighed against documented, public evidence.

scenario: A report is described as "leaked" and "reveals the truth." You cannot find the original document or any statement from the alleged source confirming it.

evidenceCards.title: Summary of "leak"
evidenceCards.snippet: Summarises what the leak supposedly says; no document, no named leaker.

evidenceCards.title: Official denial
evidenceCards.snippet: The organisation states it has no record of such a document and no comment.

options.text: Leaks are always true
options.explanation: Alleged leaks must be verified; not every claim of a leak is genuine.

options.text: Withhold judgment until the document is produced or independently verified
options.explanation: Unverifiable leaks cannot be treated as fact.

options.text: The denial proves the leak is true
options.explanation: Denials do not logically prove the opposite.

options.text: Share the summary so others can verify
options.explanation: Sharing unverifiable claims spreads potential misinformation.

takeaway: Unverifiable "leaks" should not be treated as established fact.

scenario: You notice that your main news source consistently frames one political party positively and another negatively, even when covering similar events.

evidenceCards.title: Your usual source
evidenceCards.snippet: Headlines and tone differ clearly by party; same type of event covered differently.

evidenceCards.title: Alternative source
evidenceCards.snippet: Different framing; some overlap in facts but different emphasis and tone.

options.text: Ignore the pattern; your source is always right
options.explanation: Consistent bias can distort your view; awareness is important.

options.text: Consciously diversify sources to get multiple framings and check facts
options.explanation: Diversifying sources improves balance and fact-checking.

options.text: Assume the other side is always wrong
options.explanation: Tribal reasoning undermines critical thinking.

options.text: Stop consuming news entirely
options.explanation: Avoiding news is not the same as consuming it critically.

takeaway: Noticing consistent bias is the first step; diversifying sources improves judgment.

scenario: Two studies conflict: one finds a link between X and Y (small sample, single country); another finds no link (larger, multi-country, pre-registered).

evidenceCards.title: Study A
evidenceCards.snippet: Single country, n=200, found association; authors note limitations and call for replication.

evidenceCards.title: Study B
evidenceCards.snippet: Multi-country, n=20,000, pre-registered; no significant association after adjustment.

options.text: Study A is right because it found something
options.explanation: Finding an effect in a small sample is weaker than no effect in a large, pre-registered study.

options.text: Weight Study B more: larger sample, pre-registered, multi-country
options.explanation: Methodological strength and scale support weighting B more.

options.text: They cancel out so we know nothing
options.explanation: Evidence can be weighted by quality; they do not simply cancel.

options.text: Pick the study that supports your prior belief
options.explanation: That is confirmation bias, not evidence weighting.

takeaway: Weigh studies by design, scale, and pre-registration; larger, pre-registered studies are stronger.

scenario: A claim states that "they" knew in advance about an event and did nothing. The only evidence is that a generic risk assessment once mentioned a similar scenario.

evidenceCards.title: Claim
evidenceCards.snippet: They knew. The 2019 report mentioned something like this. They did nothing.

evidenceCards.title: 2019 risk assessment
evidenceCards.snippet: Lists many hypothetical risks; no specific prediction of this event; no evidence of inaction.

options.text: Mentioning a risk in a list means they knew this would happen
options.explanation: Generic risk lists do not equate to specific foreknowledge.

options.text: Distinguish between generic risk awareness and specific foreknowledge + inaction
options.explanation: Critical thinking requires distinguishing levels of knowledge and evidence of inaction.

options.text: Any prior mention proves a cover-up
options.explanation: That would be over-interpreting a generic document.

options.text: Ignore the document and rely on the claim
options.explanation: Primary documents should be used to evaluate claims.

takeaway: Generic risk assessments are not proof of specific foreknowledge or inaction.

scenario: A health claim is attributed to "doctors" or "experts" with no names, institutions, or links.

evidenceCards.title: Article
evidenceCards.snippet: Experts say that [claim]. Doctors recommend [product]. No quotes, no names.

evidenceCards.title: Medical body statement
evidenceCards.snippet: Named authors, position statement, references to studies.

options.text: Trust it because it says experts
options.explanation: Anonymous "experts" cannot be verified.

options.text: Prefer sources that name experts and link to evidence
options.explanation: Named, verifiable sources are stronger than vague attribution.

options.text: Assume the medical body is lying
options.explanation: Assuming the opposite without evidence is not reasoning.

options.text: Share the article so more people see it
options.explanation: Sharing unverified claims spreads potential misinformation.

takeaway: Vague attribution to "experts" is weak; prefer named, verifiable sources.

scenario: You strongly dislike a public figure. A story appears that portrays them negatively. You have not checked other sources.

evidenceCards.title: Negative story
evidenceCards.snippet: Describes the figure in harsh terms; quotes critics; no response from the figure.

options.text: Believe it because it matches your view of the figure
options.explanation: That is confirmation bias.

options.text: Check whether the figure was given a chance to respond and seek other sources
options.explanation: Fairness and multiple sources improve judgment.

options.text: Assume they are guilty because you dislike them
options.explanation: Personal dislike is not evidence.

options.text: Share it to show others how bad they are
options.explanation: Sharing one-sided content can spread bias.

takeaway: When you have strong feelings, extra effort to check multiple sources is important.

scenario: Someone says a policy failed because "look at what happened after." You know many other factors changed at the same time.

evidenceCards.title: Claim
evidenceCards.snippet: Policy X was introduced in 2020. In 2021 we saw Y. So X caused Y.

evidenceCards.title: Timeline
evidenceCards.snippet: 2020: X introduced; also A, B, C changed. 2021: Y; also D, E changed.

options.text: Sequence proves causation
options.explanation: Correlation in time does not prove causation; other factors may explain Y.

options.text: Consider other factors that could explain Y before blaming X
options.explanation: Hypothesis testing requires considering alternative explanations.

options.text: If X happened before Y, X caused Y
options.explanation: Temporal order is necessary but not sufficient for causation.

options.text: Ignore the timeline
options.explanation: Context is essential for causal reasoning.

takeaway: After-the-fact correlation does not prove causation; consider confounding factors.
